feedingProfile.preferredNpk: high-potassium liquid (e.g. 5-10-10 or kelp); balanced 8-8-8 / 10-10-10 in ground

commonIssues: Container minimum (30 L) reflects that mature figs are 3-6 m trees grown in-ground; in pots figs actually fruit better with restricted roots, so avoid overpotting and repot only into a slightly larger container every couple of years (RHS).
Drought-sensitive: yellowing leaves and wilting signal under-watering; container figs and restricted-root plants can need daily checks in hot, dry weather (Clemson, RHS).

Frost-sensitive: significant die-back below about -9 C / 15 F; in cold zones (5-7) plant against a south-facing wall, mulch roots, or move container plants into an unheated shelter over winter (Missouri Botanical Garden, RHS).
Most cultivars recommended for home growing are parthenocarpic and self-fruitful; do NOT buy wild/Smyrna types that need fig-wasp (caprification) pollination (Clemson, Missouri Botanical Garden).
Over-feeding nitrogen, especially in colder regions, reduces cold hardiness and promotes leaf over fruit (Clemson).

source.citation: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Ficus carica (Edible Fig): USDA zones 6-9 (best 8-10), full sun to part shade, medium water, organically rich moist well-drained soil, self-fruitful/parthenocarpic, winter protection needed below 15 F, mature 10-20 ft, fruit late summer/fall.
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Ficus carica: self-fruitful, most cultivars parthenocarpic (set fruit without cross-pollination).
Clemson HGIC — Figs: How to Grow and Care for Figs in South Carolina: full sun (8 hours or more daily) required during the growing season.
Clemson HGIC — Figs: about 1 inch (25 mm) of water per week from rain or irrigation is adequate; more in hot dry periods; yellowing/wilting indicates drought stress.

Clemson HGIC — Figs: mature in-ground plants fed balanced 8-8-8 or 10-10-10 three times yearly (early April, early June, mid-July); colder-region plants fed less to preserve cold hardiness; common SC cultivars are self-fertile (female flowers, set fruit without cross-pollination).
RHS — How to grow figs: feed container figs with an organic high-potassium liquid fertiliser fortnightly (every ~14 days) throughout the growing season until figs ripen in late summer.

RHS — How to grow figs: start young figs in a pot ~30 cm (1 ft) wide and deep; figs fruit better with restricted roots so avoid overpotting and repot only into a slightly larger soil-based container every couple of years; keep evenly moist, checking daily in hot dry weather; position in a warm sunny south-facing spot; move container figs under cover or wrap them over winter (frost-sensitive).
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Ficus carica (Common Fig): grow in full sun; large fruit/ornamental tree habit; container culture in colder areas with overwintering indoors.

Confidence note — recommendedMinLiters (30 L), idealMin/Max (40/60 L), heatStressC (38 C), hotWeatherMultiplier (2), and rainSkipThresholdMm (25, from Clemson's 1 inch/week) are conservative app estimates. Extension sources give pot size in diameter (RHS ~30 cm starter pot ≈ 14 L; figs are otherwise large in-ground 3-6 m trees) and water as ~1 inch/week rather than an exact pot volume or interval; no source gives a numeric upper heat-stress temperature. Values chosen for a realistic productive container fig that intentionally keeps roots somewhat restricted per RHS.
